title: A Word To Texas Jack
content:
آ آ آ آ Texas Jack, you are amusinï؟½. By Lord Harry, how I laughed
آ آ آ آ When I seen yer rig and saddle with its bulwarks fore-and-aft;
آ آ آ آ Holy smoke! In such a saddle how the dickens can yer fall?
آ آ آ آ Why, I seen a gal ride bareback with no bridle on at all!
آ آ آ آ Gosh! so-help-me! strike-me-balmy! if a bit oï؟½ scenery
آ آ آ آ Like ter you in all yer rig-out on the earth I ever see!
آ آ آ آ How Iï؟½d like ter see a bushman use yer fixins, Texas Jack;
آ آ آ آ On the remnant of a saddle he can ride to hell and back.
آ آ آ آ Why, I heerd a mother screaminï؟½ when her kid went tossinï؟½ by
آ آ آ آ Ridinï؟½ bareback on a bucker that had murder in his eye.
آ آ آ آ What? yer come to learn the natives how to squat on horseï؟½s back!
آ آ آ آ Learn the cornstalk ridinï؟½! Blazes!, wï؟½at yer givï؟½nï؟½us, Texas Jack?
آ آ آ آ Learn the cornstalk, what the flaminï؟½, jumptup! whereï؟½s my country gone?
آ آ آ آ Why, the cornstalkï؟½s mother often rides the day afore heï؟½s born!

آ آ آ آ You may talk about your ridinï؟½ in the city, bold anï؟½ free,
آ آ آ آ Talk oï؟½ ridinï؟½ in the city, Texas Jack, but whereï؟½d yer be
آ آ آ آ When the stock horse snorts anï؟½ bunches all ï؟½is quarters in a hump,
آ آ آ آ And the saddle climbs a sapling, anï؟½ the horse-shoes split a stump?

آ آ آ آ No, before yer teach the native you must ride without a fall
آ آ آ آ Up a gum or down a gully nigh as steep as any wall, 
آ آ آ آ You must swim the roarinï؟½ Darlinï؟½ when the flood is at its height
آ آ آ آ Bearinï؟½ down the stock anï؟½ stations to the Great Australian Bight.

آ آ آ آ You canï؟½t count the bulls anï؟½ bisons that yer copped with your lassoo, 
آ آ آ آ But a stout old myall bullock pï؟½raps ï؟½ud learn yer somethinï؟½ new;
آ آ آ آ Yerï؟½d better make yer will anï؟½ leave yer papers neat anï؟½ trim
آ آ آ آ Before yer make arrangements for the lassooinï؟½ of him;
آ آ آ آ Ere you ï؟½nï؟½ yer horse is catsmeat, fittinï؟½ fate for sich galoots,
آ آ آ آ And yer saddleï؟½s turned to laces like we put in blucher boots.

آ آ آ آ And yer say yer death on Injins! Weï؟½ve got somethinï؟½in yer line, 
آ آ آ آ If yer think your fitinï؟½s ekal to the likes of Tommy Ryan.
آ آ آ آ Take yer karkass up to Queensland where the allygators chew
آ آ آ آ And the carpet-snake is handy with his tail for a lassoo;

آ آ آ آ Ride across the hazy regins where the lonely emus wail
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ yeï؟½ll find the blackï؟½ll track yer while yer lookinï؟½ for his trail;
آ آ آ آ He can track yer without stoppinï؟½ for a thousand miles or more, 
آ آ آ آ Come again, and he will show yer where yer spit the year before.
آ آ آ آ But yerï؟½d best be mighty careful, youï؟½ll be sorry you kem here
آ آ آ آ When yer skewered to the fakements of yer saddle with a spear, 
آ آ آ آ When the boomerang is sailinï؟½ in the air, may heaven help yer!
آ آ آ آ It will cut yer head off goinï؟½, anï؟½ come back again and skelp yer.

آ آ آ آ P.S., As poet and as Yankee I will greet you, Texas Jack,
آ آ آ آ For it isnï؟½t no ill-feelinï؟½ that is gettinï؟½ up my back,
آ آ آ آ But I wonï؟½t see this land crowded by each Yank and British cuss
آ آ آ آ Who takes it in his head to come a-civilisinï؟½ us.
آ آ آ آ So if you feel like shootinï؟½ now, donï؟½t let yer pistol cough, 
آ آ آ آ (Our Government is very free at chokinï؟½ fellers off);
آ آ آ آ And though on your great continent thereï؟½s misery in the towns
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ not a few untitled lords and kings without their crowns,
آ آ آ آ I will admit your countrymen is busted big, anï؟½ free,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ great on ekal rites of men and great on liberty;

آ آ آ آ I will admit yer fathers punched the gory tyrantï؟½s head,
آ آ آ آ But then weï؟½ve got our heroes, too, the diggers that is dead, 
آ آ آ آ The plucky men of Ballarat who toed the scratch right well
آ آ آ آ And broke the nose of Tyranny and made his peepers swell
آ آ آ آ For yankinï؟½ Lib.ï؟½s gold tresses in the roarinï؟½ days gone by,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ doublinï؟½ up his dirty fist to black her bonny eye;
آ آ آ آ So when it comes to ridinï؟½ mokes, or hoistinï؟½ out the Chow,
آ آ آ آ Or stickinï؟½ up for labourï؟½s rights, we donï؟½t want showinï؟½ how.
آ آ آ آ They come to learn us cricket in the days of long ago,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ Hanlan come from Canada to learn us how to row,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ ï؟½doctorsï؟½ come from ï؟½Frisco just to learn us how to skite,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ ï؟½pugsï؟½ from all the lands on earth to learn us how to fight;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ when they go, as like or not, we find weï؟½re taken in,
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½ve left behind no larninï؟½, but theyï؟½ve carried off our tin.
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
